name: Sierra de la Muela and Castillitos Battery Loop
name_original: Batería de Castillitos y Las Antenas — vuelta por el Parque Regional Sierra de la Muela, Cabo Tiñoso y Roldán
description: This difficult 42.2-mile road cycling route takes you through the Sierra de la Muela, Cabo Tiñoso y Roldán, with stunning views and historic
region_intro: Road cycling around Cartagena offers diverse terrain, from the flat coastal roads along the Caribbean Sea to rural landscapes with gentle climbs. The historic Walled City provides a unique urban cycling experience through colonial architecture. Routes extending beyond the city often feature wide road shoulders, contrasting with the more intense traffic within urban areas. The region's natural features include coastal paths, salt flats, and regional parks, providing varied backdrops for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around Cartagena The most popular road cycling route is Salinas de Marchamalo y La Manga del Mar Menor — from Cabo de Palos, a 25.3 miles (40.7 km) trail that takes 2 hours 3 minutes to complete. This moderate route features coastal views and passes through salt flats. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is La Unión - Portmán - Parque Calblanque — circular desde Islas Menores por Eurovelo 8, a moderate 20.6 miles (33.2 km) path. This route explores a regional park and is part of the Eurovelo 8 network. Local road cyclists also love the Bateria de Castillitos and Las Antenas - return through the Regional Park Sierra de la Muela, Cabo Tiñoso and Roldan, a 42.2 miles (68.0 km) trail leading through a regional park with significant climbs, often completed in about 3 hours 44 minutes. Road cycling around Cartagena is defined by coastal paths, rural roads, and historic urban areas. The network offers options for different ability levels, from flat rides to routes with challenging climbs. The routes in Cartagena are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 130 reviews. More than 5,500 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cartagena's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes can I explore around Cartagena?
A: There are over 240 road cycling routes available around Cartagena, offering a wide variety of experiences for different skill levels. You'll find everything from flat coastal rides to routes with significant climbs.

Q: What kind of terrain and difficulty can I expect on road cycling routes in Cartagena?
A: Cartagena's road cycling routes offer diverse terrain. Within the historic center and along the coast, you'll find mostly flat and accessible paths. Routes extending further inland or into regional parks can feature gentle to challenging climbs. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009090425/bateria-de-castillitos-and-las-antenas-return-through-the-regional-park-sierra-de-la-muela-cabo-tinoso-and-roldan" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bateria de Castillitos and Las Antenas route&lt;/a&gt; is considered difficult due to its significant elevation gain, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1010640889/salinas-de-marchamalo-y-la-manga-del-mar-menor-from-cabo-de-palos" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salinas de Marchamalo y La Manga del Mar Menor&lt;/a&gt; route is a moderate ride with coastal views.

Q: What are some scenic natural features or landmarks I can see while cycling around Cartagena?
A: Road cycling routes around Cartagena offer a blend of coastal beauty and historical sites. You can cycle along the stunning Avenida Santander, explore the unique salt flats near La Manga del Mar Menor, or venture into regional parks like Calblanque. Historic fortifications, such as the impressive San Felipe de Barajas Castle, are also prominent. For specific viewpoints, consider routes that pass by &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1054778" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Fuente&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2939367" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roldán Peak&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes in the Cartagena area?
A: Yes, there are several circular road cycling routes available. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1008654090/la-union-portman-parque-calblanque-circular-desde-islas-menores-por-eurovelo-8" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Unión - Portmán - Parque Calblanque circular route&lt;/a&gt;, which is part of the Eurovelo 8 network and explores a regional park. These routes are great for experiencing diverse landscapes without retracing your path.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling in Cartagena?
A: Cartagena has a tropical climate, meaning it's hot and humid year-round. The wet season typically runs from May to June and October to November. For the most comfortable experience, early morning rides are highly recommended to avoid the peak heat and humidity, regardless of the season.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Cartagena?
A: The road cycling routes in Cartagena are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 130 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, from flat coastal paths to challenging climbs, and the unique experience of cycling through historic urban areas and natural parks.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling routes in Cartagena?
A: While the historic center offers flat terrain suitable for families, traffic can be intense. For a more relaxed family experience, consider routes along the coast, such as parts of Avenida Santander, especially on Sunday mornings when sections are closed to cars. The Bocagrande area also features loops with mostly well-paved surfaces suitable for all fitness levels.

Q: Can I find routes that include cafes or places to stop along the way?
A: Many routes, especially those passing through or near urban areas and coastal towns, will have opportunities to stop at cafes and restaurants. While specific routes aren't tagged for cafes, exploring areas like Bocagrande or the historic center will provide ample options for refreshments.

Q: What are the road conditions like for cycling in Cartagena?
A: Within the city, especially on tourist routes, many areas have well-paved surfaces. However, some segments outside the city might be unpaved or challenging. Traffic in Cartagena can be heavy, particularly in the city center, so cyclists should be highly aware of their surroundings. Cycling early in the morning can help avoid the heaviest traffic.

Q: Are there any challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for experienced riders seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1009090425/bateria-de-castillitos-and-las-antenas-return-through-the-regional-park-sierra-de-la-muela-cabo-tinoso-and-roldan" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bateria de Castillitos and Las Antenas&lt;/a&gt; offer significant climbs and cover a distance of over 67 km. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19393158" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cedacero Viewpoint – Road to Mazarrón loop&lt;/a&gt;, which features substantial elevation gain over 75 km.

Q: How can I find parking or access road cycling routes via public transport in Cartagena?
A: Parking availability varies, especially in the busy city center. It's often easier to find parking outside the immediate historic core. For public transport, local buses serve various parts of Cartagena, but carrying a road bike on them might be challenging. Consider starting your ride from areas with easier parking access or accommodation that offers bike storage.

Q: Are there any specific safety considerations for road cycling in Cartagena?
A: While Colombia has improved safety, it's always wise to research routes carefully, especially when venturing outside well-known tourist areas. Traffic can be intense in urban areas, so maintaining awareness is crucial. Cycling with others and utilizing local knowledge for route planning is recommended for a safer experience.
tips: There are beautiful views of La Azohía and Isla Plana from the Mirador del Cedacero. If you cycle through the area it is a mandatory stop.
Popular destination for road bike lovers. Until the Cerro de los Siete Cucones or popularly known as Las Antenas, we have a beautiful climb with magnificent views of the sea.

The Castillitos battery was armed between 1933 and 1936. Recently rehabilitated for your visit, walking through this military complex and contemplating its peaceful views of the Mediterranean and its ships is simply impressive.

faqs.question: How difficult is this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a difficult road ride, requiring a very good level of fitness. It features significant elevation gain and some challenging terrain, making it best suited for experienced cyclists.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily asphalt (about 60.8 km) and paved (around 7.13 km). However, be prepared for unpaved sections that can be difficult to ride. The road leading to Batería de los Castillitos, in particular, is narrow, winding, bumpy, and may have potholes, potentially requiring you to push your bike in some parts.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The estimated duration for this challenging 68 km route is approximately 3 hours and 45 minutes of active cycling time. This can vary depending on your fitness level and how much time you spend enjoying the viewpoints.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: While the exact starting point with dedicated parking isn't specified, many cyclists begin their journey from nearby towns or accessible points within the Regional Park. It's advisable to research parking options in areas like La Azohía or near the entrance to the park roads leading towards Batería de los Castillitos.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Key highlights include the impressive coastal artillery fortification Batería de los Castillitos, the panoramic Cedacero Viewpoint, and the stunning View from Las Antenas (Cerro de los Siete Cucones). The route also passes through beautiful natural landscapes of the Regional Park Sierra de la Muela, Cabo Tiñoso and Roldan.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for cycling in the Regional Park Sierra de la Muela, Cabo Tiñoso and Roldan?
faqs.answer: Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads and trails within the Parque Regional de la Sierra de la Muela, Cabo Tiñoso y Roldán. However, always respect local regulations, especially concerning protected areas and historical sites like Batería de los Castillitos.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The route can be enjoyed for much of the year, but spring and autumn offer the most pleasant temperatures for cycling. Summers can be very hot, and while winters are mild, some sections might be exposed to strong winds. Early mornings are often recommended for peaceful rides and to witness beautiful sunrises.

faqs.question: Is this road cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: As a challenging road cycling route, it is generally not recommended for dogs, especially given the distance, elevation, and potential for unpaved or rough sections. If you do bring a dog, ensure they are well-trained, can keep pace, and are always on a leash, respecting park rules and other users.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other official cycling trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route intersects with several other trails. Notable sections include parts of the Transmurciana 07, Sendero del Mediterráneo - Murcia, and CIMA MU11: Cabo Tiñoso.

faqs.question: What essential gear should I bring for this challenging ride?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and remoteness, it's crucial to bring plenty of water, energy snacks, a repair kit (including spare tubes and a pump), appropriate cycling attire, sun protection, and a fully charged phone. A GPS device or a downloaded map is also highly recommended due to the winding nature of some roads.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as difficult and requires a very good fitness level, making it unsuitable for families with young children or beginner cyclists. The significant elevation gain, long distance, and challenging terrain, including potentially rough road sections, are best tackled by experienced road cyclists.

waypoints.name: View from Las Antenas (Cerro de los Siete Cucones)
